description: Explains how pytest builds configuration, discovers and controls plugins, loads conftest files, and exposes hook-based extension points during startup and test execution.
document:
Pytest treats configuration, plugin loading, and hook execution as one startup system. The central object is `Config`, defined in `src/_pytest/config/__init__.py`, which stores the parsed command-line namespace, the selected root directory and config file, ini-style values, cleanup callbacks, and the hook relay exposed to the rest of the run. A fresh run is prepared by creating a plugin manager, registering built-in plugins, then asking the command-line parse hook to return the initialized config. This means ordinary options, config-file settings, and extension hooks are all available through one object that is passed to plugins, nodes, sessions, and fixtures.

Command-line and ini registration are handled by `Parser` in `src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py`. Plugins usually participate through the add-option hook, where they can add argparse-style flags or declare config-file values. The parser records option destinations so later code can ask the config object for an option by its destination or by an option spelling. For config-file values, the parser records the declared type, default, and any aliases. When code asks the config object for an ini value, aliases resolve to the canonical name, command-line overrides win over file values, and the selected value is converted according to the type that was declared by the plugin.

The file search and root-directory decision live in `src/_pytest/config/findpaths.py`. Pytest looks for supported pytest configuration files upward from the invocation inputs, prefers the first usable file according to its search order, and may fall back to an empty project file or to a common ancestor when no explicit config is present. The loader distinguishes legacy string-oriented ini mode from native TOML mode. In ini mode pytest coerces text into the declared shape; in TOML mode it preserves native values and validates that they match the expected type. The same module parses override options as synthetic config values with override origin, which is why they take precedence later.

Plugin management is implemented by `PytestPluginManager`, a pytest-specific subclass of pluggy's manager. It installs the hook specifications from `src/_pytest/hookspec.py`, registers itself, and then handles several plugin sources: built-in modules, explicit command-line plugin arguments, installed entry points, the plugin environment variable, and local conftest files. The public docs describe this same order and also explain the controls for disabling behavior. A plugin can be blocked with a command-line no-plugin form, while automatic third-party entry point loading can be disabled with either the dedicated option or the matching environment variable. Explicit plugin loading through command-line arguments or the plugin environment variable remains available for controlled startup.

Local conftest files are treated as plugins with directory scope. During initial loading, pytest derives anchor paths from command-line test arguments or fallback collection paths, walks parent directories, imports applicable conftest modules, and registers them under their file path. The manager caches conftest lists per directory so repeated lookups are cheap, and it uses the nearest applicable conftest first when retrieving per-directory values. The implementation also rejects attempts to block a conftest file through the normal plugin flag, because conftest files are discovered by path rather than by plugin name. A non-top-level conftest that declares plugin dependencies after configuration has begun is rejected, because such dependencies affect the whole run rather than only that subtree.

Hooks are the contract that makes plugins useful. The hook specifications in `src/_pytest/hookspec.py` document which startup, collection, and runtest events plugins may implement, and whether a hook is historic or stops at the first non-empty result. The writing-hooks guide explains pytest's validation rule: implementation argument names must match the specification, but a plugin may omit arguments it does not need. That pruning lets pytest add new named parameters without breaking old plugins. Hook wrappers run around ordinary implementations, and ordering hints can move implementations earlier or later within their kind. The tests in `testing/test_pluginmanager.py` cover custom hook registration, hook use from add-option handling, historic plugin-registration calls, and conftest hook behavior, while `testing/test_config.py` covers config-file precedence, ini aliases, plugin autoload disabling, required plugins, and non-top-level conftest dependency errors.
